summary:
The Lewis and Clark Expedition (1804–06), formally known as the Corps of Discovery, was the first American overland expedition to the Pacific Coast and back. Commissioned by President Thomas Jefferson to explore the recently acquired [[event:2601|Louisiana Purchase]] territory and find a route to the Pacific, the expedition covered approximately 12,800 kilometers, catalogued hundreds of plant and animal species previously unknown to Western science, and established American claims to the Pacific Northwest.

The expedition was led by Meriwether Lewis, Jefferson's personal secretary, and William Clark, an experienced frontiersman. Starting from Camp Dubois near St. Louis in May 1804, the Corps of Discovery traveled up the Missouri River, wintered with the Mandan people in present-day North Dakota, and crossed the Rocky Mountains guided by the Shoshone woman Sacagawea, whose knowledge of the terrain and languages proved invaluable. They reached the Pacific Coast at the mouth of the Columbia River in November 1805, wintered there, and returned to St. Louis in September 1806.

The expedition produced the first systematic scientific survey of the American West, including detailed maps, natural history specimens, and ethnographic observations of the many Native American nations encountered along the route. It established American awareness of the Pacific Northwest and asserted the U.S. claim to the Oregon Territory (later resolved with Britain in 1846). It also, by demonstrating the enormous extent and richness of the continent's interior, amplified the American ambition for westward expansion that would drive [[event:2566|Manifest Destiny]] over the following decades — with devastating consequences for the Native peoples the expedition had so carefully documented.
